name: Quantum measurement problem
summary: Standard quantum mechanics has two distinct dynamics: unitary Schrodinger evolution between measurements, and instantaneous projection onto an eigenstate during measurement. The measurement step is a postulate rather than a derivable consequence of unitary dynamics; it raises the question of where the quantum-classical boundary lies and what selects the preferred basis ('pointer states') along which collapse occurs. The puzzle has structural implications for any theory aiming to be a complete description of physics including observers.

characteristic_numbers.note: Environment-induced decoherence time tau_D depends on system-environment coupling; quantum coherence is lost in 10^-20 - 10^-13 s for macroscopic objects. Decoherence chooses pointer states but does not explain unique outcome.

principles_stressed.note: Unitary evolution alone doesn't reduce a superposition to a unique measured outcome; either an additional non-unitary postulate (Copenhagen), an environmental decoherence + many-worlds branching (Everett), an objective collapse (GRW, Penrose), or a hidden-variable + guidance equation (Bohm) is needed.

Bell-inequality violations (Aspect 1982; loophole-free 2015) demonstrate quantum non-locality at correlated-measurement level. How non-locality is reconciled with relativistic causality varies by interpretation.

history: Born (1926) interpretation of |psi|^2 as probability. Schrodinger's cat (1935). Copenhagen (Bohr-Heisenberg) vs Bohm (1952) hidden variables vs Everett (1957) relative-state. Decoherence program (Joos-Zeh 1985; Zurek 1991) shows how classical-looking outcomes emerge from environmental entanglement. Bell (1964) inequalities and Aspect (1982) experiments rule out local hidden variables. Modern resolution: decoherence + interpretation choice; no consensus.

references.citation: Zurek, 'Decoherence, einselection, and the quantum origins of the classical', Rev. Mod. Phys. 75, 715 (2003)
